coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-controlled gold nanozyme sensor for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-channel detection of methamphetamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Chen
Zhang, Yida
Zhang, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Methamphetamine (METH) is the second abused drug which affects abusers’ health and induces social crimes, developing novel methods with high sensitivity and selectivity for METH detecting is still challenging. In this paper, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel sensor for METH has been constructed. We combine the enzyme-mimic catalytic activity of gold nanoparticles (GNPs) with high target specificity of METH aptamer to create a nanosensor (Apt-GNP), in the presence of METH, the absorption of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (OxTMB) at 650 nm enhanced with METH concentration increasing, while the absorption characteristic peak of GNPs at 530 nm remained almost unchanged. The ratio of A650nm/A530nm and METH concentration had a good linear relationship when METH concentration was in the range of 5–50 μM, and the corresponding linear equation is A650nm/A530nm = 0.00727CMETH (μM) + 0.783 with R2 = 0.997 and LOD = 0.40 μM (LOD = 3σ/s, n = 11). Interestingly, the fluorescence emission of Rhodamine B (RB) overlaps with the absorption spectrum of OxTMB which represents the content of METH and the fluorescence signal of RB can be quenched through internal filtering effect (IEF). Hence, when RB was doped to the detection system, the decay of RB fluorescence can reflect the concentrations change of METH. Accordingly, the linear equation is F/FR = –0.00751CMETH (μM) + 0.895 with R2 = 0.993 and LOD = 0.40 μM, where F was the fluorescence of the analytical solution at 580 nm with METH and FR was fluorescence of RB control solution. The dual-channel sensor can measure METH in serum and artificial urine successfully which is potential to be applied in drug-using crime sites and provide direct evidence to law enforcement officials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methamphetamine
Gold nanoparticles
Methamphetamine aptamer
Internal filtering effect
Dual-channel detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of GNPs with METH aptamer (Apt-GNP)
Analysis of diluted artificial urine samples and human serum
Investigation of the preparation and enzyme-like characteristics of GNPs and Apt-GNP
Study on the mechanism of fluorescence signal change
Analysis of diluted human serum and artificial urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel detection for METH.

TEM image of GNPs (A) and Apt-GNP (B); (C) Zeta potentials of GNPs, METH aptamer and Apt-GNP; (D) Comparison of UV–Vis absorption spectra of different solutions. The inset was the photo of the solutions, 1–4 were solutions of GNPs; GNPs, TMB and H2O2; Apt-GNP, TMB and H2O2; and Apt-GNP, METH, TMB and H2O2, respectively.

(A) Internal filtration effect between RB (RB fluorescence, red line) and OxTMB (absorption spectrum of OxTMB, black line); (B) Fluorescence emission of different solutions, black line is RB control solution; pink line is RB in Apt-GNP solution, green line is RB in Apt-GNP and METH (25 μM) solution; blue line is RB in GNPs solution; (C) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of RB (black curve), RB in GNPs solution (red curve), and RB in Apt-GNP solution (green curve); (D) Absorption spectrum of RB control solution (green curve), RB in GNPs solution (black curve), and RB in Apt-GNP solution (red curve).

UV–Vis spectra (A) and fluorescence emission spectra (C) with different concentrations of METH in Apt-GNP solutions. The linear equations of the ratio of A650nm/A530nm (B) and values of F/FR (D) with varying METH concentrations, n = 3. All measurements are performed according measurement procedure in the main text.

Specificity of the dual-channel assay for METH detection. Absorption at 650 nm (A) and the value of F/FR (B) in the presence of METH (25 μM) or interference (50 μM), n = 3. All measurements are performed according measurement procedure in the main text.
Comparison of performance of different METH detection assays.
Determination of METH in actual samples by standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-controlled gold nanozyme sensor for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-channel detection of methamphetamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Gansu Province Key Laboratory of Evidence Science Techniques Research and Application, Gansu University of Political Science and Law, Lanzhou, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Gansu Province Key Laboratory of Evidence Science Techniques Research and Application
Gansu University of Political Science and Law

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methamphetamine (METH) is the second abused drug which affects abusers’ health and induces social crimes, developing novel methods with high sensitivity and selectivity for METH detecting is still challenging. In this paper, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel sensor for METH has been constructed. We combine the enzyme-mimic catalytic activity of gold nanoparticles (GNPs) with high target specificity of METH aptamer to create a nanosensor (Apt-GNP), in the presence of METH, the absorption of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (OxTMB) at 650 nm enhanced with METH concentration increasing, while the absorption characteristic peak of GNPs at 530 nm remained almost unchanged. The ratio of A650nm/A530nm and METH concentration had a good linear relationship when METH concentration was in the range of 5–50 μM, and the corresponding linear equation is A650nm/A530nm = 0.00727CMETH (μM) + 0.783 with R2 = 0.997 and LOD = 0.40 μM (LOD = 3σ/s, n = 11). Interestingly, the fluorescence emission of Rhodamine B (RB) overlaps with the absorption spectrum of OxTMB which represents the content of METH and the fluorescence signal of RB can be quenched through internal filtering effect (IEF). Hence, when RB was doped to the detection system, the decay of RB fluorescence can reflect the concentrations change of METH. Accordingly, the linear equation is F/FR = –0.00751CMETH (μM) + 0.895 with R2 = 0.993 and LOD = 0.40 μM, where F was the fluorescence of the analytical solution at 580 nm with METH and FR was fluorescence of RB control solution. The dual-channel sensor can measure METH in serum and artificial urine successfully which is potential to be applied in drug-using crime sites and provide direct evidence to law enforcement officials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, drug abuse is a global concern, one such illicit drug as methamphetamine (METH) is an illegal drug abused worldwide, after being ingested by human body, it releases a large amount of neurotransmitter dopamine, producing a sense of pleasure, which extremely affects the central nervous system to the abuser [1,2]. After being metabolized by the the user, METH and its metabolites, such as amphetamine and hydroxyamphetamine can be detected in the urine, blood, saliva, and hair [3]. Therefore, the detection of METH in different biological samples is very necessary. Existing detection methods for METH mainly include gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [4–7]. Unsatisfactory, these methods require complex sample pre-treatment processes in most cases and are difficult to carry out outside the laboratory. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a drug detection method with high sensitivity, good accuracy, simple operation, and low cost to achieve point of care tests (POCT) for METH.

Today, sensors based on nanomaterials which have advantages such as large specific surface area, easy to control size and modify, and good optical property are developing rapidly and have been applied to detect of small molecule such as illegal drugs, ions, proteins, and nucleic acids [8–10]. In particular, gold nanoparticles (GNPs) have received numerous attentions as its excellent enzyme-like catalytic property. In the presence of GNPs, TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) can be oxidized to OxTMB by H2O2 rapidly and the solution color turns characteristic blue [11]. Utilizing the enzyme-like catalytic property of GNPs, researchers had established many nanosensors to colorimetric detect various substances [12–14]. According to recent research, the presence of nitrogen atoms within the single-strand DNA (ssDNA) and GNPs has unique interaction and ssDNA aptamer is easy to attach to the surface of GNPs, which can reduce the number of catalytic sites on the GNPs surface and the enzymatic property would be weakened [15,16]. After adding the corresponding target of these aptamers, due to the specific strong binding between the aptamer and the target, the aptamer can separate from GNPs surface and the catalytic property of GNPs is restored. Through this principle, some new colorimetric methods to detect small molecules, proteins and viruses had been developed [17–19].

However, the current detection methods based on above strategy are generally only carried out on colorimetric channel, and there are few reports about METH detection, let alone using dual-channel method to recognize METH. Single-channel response is susceptible to interference by various factors, which limits their application in complex biological samples [20,21]. On the contrary, dual-channel detection strategy can display the advantages of each channel, and the detection results of two channels can be self-corrected, consequently improves the accuracy of the detection assay. Hence, it is indispensable to develop a dual-channel method for METH detection. Internal filtration effect (IFE) refers to the excitation or emission of fluorescent substance had a certain overlap with some substance, resulting in fluorescence quenching [22]. IEF can be used to establish a relationship between the absorbing substance and the fluorescent substance, which can increase the sensitivity of quantitative analysis. Additionally, the IFE mechanism typically requires no surface modification or any covalent connection between the receptor and the donor [23]. Therefore, it is a smart choice to combine IEF with colorimetric method to realize dual-channel signal output. In the current work, the produced amount of OxTMB represents the concentration of METH and OxTMB can quench the fluorescence signal of RB through IEF [24], hence, the change of fluorescence signal of RB also reflects the changes of METH concentration when RB is introduced into this assay. Thus, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel detection method for METH can be established.

Herein, we developed a new dual-channel strategy based on GNPs and IEF for METH detection. As shown in Fig. 1 , due to the coordination interaction between the nitrogenous bases of the unfolded ssDNA and GNPs, METH aptamer can occupy GNPs surface to produce Apt-GNP, which reduced the catalytic site of GNPs. When introducing METH, due to the stronger binding force between the aptamer and METH, the aptamer detached from GNPs surface and GNPs regained its catalytic performance, in the acidic detection system with TMB and H2O2, OxTMB was produced resulting in a blue color change. Utilizing the IEF between OxTMB and RB, RB was doped into the detection system to achieve the fluorescence channel signal change. Briefly, in the presence of METH, OxTMB is generated and the solution shows obvious blue, meanwhile, the fluorescence signal is weakened as the IEF between OxTMB and RB. Based on this, we used economic reagent (such as H2O2, TMB, and RB) to construct a practical colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel strategy for METH measurement.

Standard substance of methamphetamine (METH), cocaine, heroin and morphine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Beijing, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) and Rhodamine B (RB) were gotten from Heowns reagent (Tianjin, China). 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate reagent kit and 30 % w/w hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were bought from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology, Ltd. Inorganic salts of NaCl, MgSO4, CaCl2 were gotten from Guangfu Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). The METH aptamer sequence was custom synthesized and obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China), and the sequence is 5′-ACG GTT GCA AGT GGG ACT CTG GTA GGC TGG GTT AAT TTG G-3′ [25]. L-lysine, aspartic acid, urea and potassium hydroxide (KOH) were from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Deionized water used in all the experiments were acquired from Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm).

The structural morphologies of GNPs and Apt-GNP were analyzed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (TF20, FEI, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry was performed by an UV–Vis spectrophotometer lambda 35 (Perkin Elmer, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (FL8500, Perkin Elmer, USA). The photos of solution color were taken through a smartphone. The zeta potentials of GNPs, METH aptamer and Apt-GNP were tested by ZEN 3600 (Malvern, UK). The concentration of gold in the prepared GNPs solution was determined using an atomic absorption spectrometer (contrAA700, Analytik Jena AG, Germany).

GNPs were synthesized using tyrosine amino acid as a reducing and capping agent according to reported literature [26]. Briefly, 150 mL aqueous solution comprising 0.1 mM L-tyrosine and 1 mM KOH was heated to boil. Under alkaline boiling conditions, 0.2 mM equivalent of [AuCl4 -] ions were added to the above solution with subsequent boiling for further 5 min. When the solution color became ruby-red indicating GNPs were produced. To prepare concentrated GNPs solution, the solution was continued boiling to reduce the volume to 15 mL. Further, the concentrated solution of GNPs was dialyzed overnight with deionized water through 12 kDa molecular weight cellulose dialysis membranes, followed by exchange of water twice to remove the excess amount of KOH, metal ions and unbound tyrosine potentially. The concentration of gold in GNPs was determined using an atomic absorption spectrometer and followed by preparation of an aqueous GNP stock solution with 1 mM equivalent of gold. UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to characterize the absorption of GNPs and there is a characteristic absorption peak of GNPs at 530 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of GNPs with METH aptamer (Apt-GNP)
ce:section.ce:para: First, METH aptamer was heated to 92 °C for 10 min, followed by snap-chilling on ice for 5 min and bringing it back to the room temperature. Following, different concentrations of METH aptamers (0, 0.25 μM, 0.50 μM, 1.0 μM, 1.5 μM, 2.0 μM, 3.0 μM) were incubated with a fixed concentration of GNPs (200 µM) for 10 min, the optimal concentration of METH aptamer is determined by measuring the absorption value at 650 nm of OxTMB. The lower the absorption value at 650 nm, the greater impact of METH aptamer on the enzymatic catalytic property of GNPs, thereby selecting the optimal concentration of the aptamer to obtain the final Apt-GNP.

In a 220 µL reaction volume, 50 μL Apt-GNP solution, 50 μL TMB and 50 μL METH with different concentrations or other analytes were added into the tubes. Then 50 μL of 0.2 mol·L−1 H2O2 and 20 μL RB solution (1 μM) were added and incubated for 15 min, all solutions were ensured to be tested under pH = 4 conditions. Subsequently, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of the reaction system were measured respectively. In the fluorescence detection, FR represents the fluorescence intensity of RB control solution, which remains unchanged and can be used as a reference, and F is the fluorescence intensity of analytical solution at 580 nm with METH. Using F/FR to establish the linear equation can effectively avoid the interference caused by changes in instrument parameters.

To obtain a sensitive dual-channel sensor for METH determination, a series of parameters were optimized as following: (a) METH aptamer concentration was investigated from 0 to 3.0 μM; (b) temperature was investigated from 20℃ to 55 ℃; (c) H2O2 concentration was investigated from 0.05 mol·L-1 to 0.5 mol·L-1; (d) incubation time was investigated from 5 min to 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of diluted artificial urine samples and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The detection performance of the dual-channel sensor was investigated by artificial urine and human serum as real samples. For investigation of artificial urine, urea (20.0 g), sodium chloride (8.0 g), magnesium sulfate (0.8 g), calcium chloride (0.3 g) and deionized water (970 mL) were mixed to make artificial urine solution. Then, the artificial urine solutions were spiked with 6 μM and 30 μM of METH for colorimetric and fluorescence detection, respectively. The commercial enclosed human serum (purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology) was diluted with 25-fold and 50-fold, then 6 μM and 30 μM of METH were added severally. These spiked solutions were mixed with Apt-GNP, TMB, H2O2 and RB according to Measurement procedure for colorimetric and fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the preparation and enzyme-like characteristics of GNPs and Apt-GNP
ce:section.ce:para: As the enzyme-like activity of GNPs is central to the sensor mechanism, we first verified the preparation and the enzyme-like characteristics of tyrosine-functionalized GNPs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the tyrosine-functionalized GNPs were regular spherical morphology of low polydispersity with an average diameter of about 20 nm. METH aptamer and GNPs were incubated for 10 min to obtain Apt-GNP and the morphology of Apt-GNP is consist with GNPs (Fig. 2B). Through zeta potential characterization, it is found that the potential decreases from −7.20 mV to −14.5 mV after the formation of Apt-GNP (Fig. 2C), which indicated METH aptamer (−9.87 mV) was successfully modified on the surface of GNPs. When TMB and H2O2 were added to GNPs solution, the solution color quickly turned characteristic blue, and there was an obvious absorption peak at 650 nm indicating the outstanding peroxidase-mimic Nanozyme activity of these GNPs (Fig. 2D). Conversely, in the Apt-GNP solution, the color change was not obvious and the absorption at 650 nm was weak, this illustrated the peroxidase-like activity of GNPs was inhibited due to the GNPs surface were shielded by METH aptamer. As demonstrated in Fig. 2D and the inset, when METH was added in Apt-GNP solution, due to the specific strong interaction between the aptamer and the target, the aptamer detached from the surface of GNPs and the Nanozyme activity of the detection system was restored which resulting in the solution color changed to blue and the peak at 650 nm became obvious. These experiments demonstrate that utilizing Apt-GNP to realize colorimetric detection of METH is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of fluorescence signal change
ce:section.ce:para: The OxTMB showed a blue color and the absorption spectrum overlaps with RB emission (Fig. 3 A), hence, the fluorescence signal of RB can be quenched by inner filter effect (IFE). As shown in Fig. 3B, in GNPs solution, the fluorescence signal of RB is quenched by large amount of OxTMB due to the exposed enzymatic catalytic site on GNPs (blue line). In the Apt-GNP system, as the enzyme activity decreases, only little amount of OxTMB is produced and no IEF happened resulting in the fluorescence signal of is still strong (pink line). When METH is present in Apt-GNP system, the enzymatic activity recovers and more OxTMB is produced again, the IEF between OxTMB and RB is existed, generating the quenching of RB fluorescence signal (green line). The signal decrease of RB fluorescence was proportional to the produced OxTMB which also depends on the amount of METH. Based on this, a fluorescence method for METH detection was constructed easily. As demonstrated in Fig. 3C, the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of RB, RB in GNPs solution, RB in Apt-GNP were basically overlaps through the fluorescence lifetime test, and the fluorescence decay lifetimes of the three curves were the same as 1.69 ns, which indicates no dynamic quenching effect (DQE) had happened [27]. Observing the UV–Vis spectra of RB, RB in GNPs solution (including TMB and H2O2) and RB in Apt-GNP solution (including TMB and H2O2), there was no other absorption peak found except for the UV–Vis absorption peaks of OxTMB and RB, which indicating no new compounds were produced (Fig. 3D) and no static quenching effect (SQE) was occurred [28]. These results indicating the quenching of RB fluorescence is just on account of IEF between OxTMB and RB.

In order to maintain the stability of the experimental results as much as possible, the following experimental conditions were optimized: different concentrations (0, 0.25 μM, 0.50 μM, 1.0 μM, 1.5 μM, 2.0 μM, 3.0 μM) of METH aptamer solution were incubated with a fixed concentration of GNPs (200 µM) to selected the optimal concentration of METH aptamer; the most suitable reaction time and temperature were measured; H2O2 concentration was investigated from 0.05 mol·L-1 to 0.5 mol·L-1. The corresponding data and figures are given in Supporting Information (Fig. S2). From the results, it was found that the following experimental conditions can achieve best results: (a) when METH aptamer was 2 µM, the obtained Apt-GNP exhibited the greatest reduction in the catalytic performance of GNPs, which is conducive to improving the sensitivity of the proposed method; (b) the optimal reaction time of the detection system was 15 min and the temperature was room temperature; (c) H2O2 concentration used in the detection system was 0.20 µM.

A single detection method is prone to false positive signals in complex substrates, which makes the establishment of multi-signal detection methods become a substitutable choice to avoid false positive signals and also allows different methods to play their own advantages. The above results prove that colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel detection of METH is a feasible scheme by utilizing the enzyme-like activity of GNPs and the IEF between RB and OxTMB. Therefore, we investigated the ability of this constructed dual-channel strategy for detecting METH under the optimal conditions. As observed in Fig. 4 A, in Apt-GNP system, the absorption at 650 nm enhanced with the increased concentration of METH, while the absorption at 530 nm (the characteristic peak of GNPs) remains essentially unchanged. Therefore, a ratiometric colorimetric method can be used to investigate the detection capability of our proposed method for METH. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the ratio of A650nm/A530nm and METH concentration had a good linear relationship when METH concentration was in the range of 5–50 μM, the corresponding linear equation is A650nm/A530nm = 0.00727CMETH (μM) + 0.783 with R2 = 0.997 and LOD = 0.40 μM (LOD = 3σ/s, n = 11). Correspondingly, the fluorescent signal value of F/FR (where FR represents fluorescence of RB control solution, F was the fluorescence of the analytical solution at 580 nm with METH) decreased gradually with METH concentrations increased, and a good linear relationship between the value of F/FR and METH concentration ranging from 5-50 μM. The corresponding linear equation is F/FR = –0.00751CMETH (μM) + 0.895 with R2 = 0.993 and LOD = 0.40 μM, (LOD = 3σ/s, n = 11) (Fig. 4D). In addition, the solution color changed with colorimetric and fluorescent signal varying (Fig. S3). The above results indicated that the dual-channel detection method for METH is practicable.

The established strategy was also compared with other methods for METH detecting and the result is demonstrated in Table 1 . It can be seen from Table 1 that the sensitivity and linear range of our strategy is both competitive. Meanwhile, it can simultaneously detect METH under channels of colorimetric and fluorescence, which can avoid the drawbacks of single channel output, such as false positive signals and susceptibility to interference, therefore improves the accuracy of test results and the capability of practical application. It is noteworthy that the dual-channel detection method is rarely reported in the field of METH testing.

The selectivity of the constructed dual-channel detection method was investigated with several addictive drugs such as cocaine, heroin, and morphine. In addition, amino acids including aspartic acid and lysine, small molecules as toluene which is similar to METH and urea were also used as potential interfering substances to explore the specificity of the assay. As shown in Fig. 5 , the signal changes in absorbance at 650 nm and F/FR were negligible when these substances were introduced. In contrast, apparent changes in fluorescent and colorimetric channels raised after METH (25 μM) were added. Furthermore, when the interference substance and METH were coexisted, the signal changes of fluorescent and colorimetric channels were little affected. These results confirmed that the selectivity and anti-interference of this dual-channel sensor were satisfactory. Apparently, the excellent selectivity and anti-interference of this method are attributed to the specific recognition between METH aptamer and METH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of diluted human serum and artificial urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicality of the proposed method, samples of commercial enclosed human serum diluted with 25 times (serum 1) and 50 times (serum 2), as well as artificial urine were spiked with different concentrations of METH and detected through our proposed method. As exhibited in Table 2 , the detected concentrations of METH in these samples between colorimetric channel and fluorescence channel were largely consistent, which proves that our proposed method has high accuracy. The recoveries of the spiked samples in the two channels were ranged from 92.5 to 104 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were lower than 7.23 %. These results indicated that the dual-channel detection strategy in this study can be applied to detect METH in serum and artificial urine, and shows potential for point-of-care testing (POCT) of METH in actual biological samples, which can provide effective evidence in drug-using crime sites.

In summary, we successfully constructed a dual-channel strategy for METH detection based on the enzyme-like catalytic properties of GNPs and IEF between OxTMB and RB. This method only requires simple operation steps and need for nonspecialized laboratory infrastructure to achieve METH detection in colorimetric and fluorescence channel simultaneously. Dual-channel signal output effectively avoids false positive signals generating and improves the accuracy of the detection system. Moreover, as the participation of METH aptamer, high specificity detection of METH was realized and this is conducive to POCT for METH. Meanwhile, the method was successfully applied to quantify METH in complex matrices, which illustrated this method is expected to be applied in METH detection in actual samples in drug-using crime sites and provide direct evidence to law enforcement officers. Through changing the specific aptamers for the target molecules, this method can be easily extended to detect of other illicit drugs or substance, which provides inspiration for establishment and application of other dual-channel detection methods.
Chen Ma: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yida Zhang: Supervision, Software. Yuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Gansu Higher Education Innovation Fund Project (No. 2021A-098), the Program of Gansu Youth Science and Technology Fund (No.22JR5RA600) and the Scientific Research Innovation Project of Gansu University of Political Science and Law (No.GZF2021XZD08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124883.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparison of methamphetamine-induced psychosis and schizophrenia: a review of positive, negative, and cognitive symptomatology
Front. Psychiatry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.A. Wearne, J.L. Cornish, A comparison of methamphetamine-induced psychosis and schizophrenia: a review of positive, negative, and cognitive symptomatology, Front. Psychiatry 9 (2018) 491, doi: 10.3389/fpsyt.2018.00491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methamphetamine: effects on the brain, gut and immune system
Pharmacol. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Prakash, K. Tangalakis, J. Antonipillai, L. Stojanovska, K. Nurgali, V. Apostolopoulo, Methamphetamine: effects on the brain, gut and immune system, Pharmacol. Res. 120 (2017) 60-67, doi: 10.1016/j.phrs.2017.03.00.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global methamphetamine trends: an evolving problem
Int. Crim. Justice Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. Stoneberg, R.K. Shukla, M.B. Magness, Global methamphetamine trends: an evolving problem, Int. Crim. Justice Rev. 28 (2018) 136-161, doi: 10.1177/1057567717730104.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High sensitive detection of methamphetamine by high-performance aptasensing platform based on nickel oxide nanoparticles anchored on mxene
Microchem. J.
